---
title: "Evolving Memory Agent Setup"
subtitle: "OpenSpace · nanobot · Ollama · WezTerm · OpenRouter · Gemini · Perplexity · Open WebUI · MCP · Remote Access"
author: "Ilya Kashnitsky"
date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
format:
  html:
    theme: minty
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    code-fold: false
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: scroll
    highlight-style: github
    embed-resources: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        body { font-size: 0.95rem; }
        h1.title { font-size: 1.8rem; }
        .callout { margin-block: 1.2rem; }
        code { font-size: 0.85em; }
        pre.sourceCode { font-size: 0.85em; }
        </style>
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
---

# Overview

This guide installs and wires together a self-evolving memory agent stack with full MCP support, a Telegram bot interface, scheduled tasks, and persistent remote access from any device. Follow each section in order.

**Stack summary:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **WezTerm** | Terminal emulator used throughout |
| **pixi** | Python environment manager |
| **Ollama** | Local LLM inference (offline models) |
| **Open WebUI** | Browser chat UI — Ollama + external APIs + MCP tools |
| **mcpo** | MCP-to-OpenAPI proxy for stdio-based MCP servers |
| **OpenRouter** | Unified API gateway for cloud models |
| **Gemini API** | Primary reasoning engine for OpenSpace |
| **Perplexity API** | Live-search tool inside the agent |
| **OpenSpace** | Self-evolving skill memory agent |
| **nanobot** | Lightweight agent runtime — Telegram bot + scheduled tasks |
| **systemd** | Keeps all services running permanently (Linux) |
| **Tailscale** | Secure remote access from phone or laptop |

::: {.callout-note}
**Two complementary agent layers:** OpenSpace and nanobot come from the same HKUDS lab but serve different purposes. OpenSpace is your skill-learning task engine (Python scripts, workflows). nanobot is your always-on conversational interface — it answers Telegram messages, runs scheduled briefings, and shares your MCP tools. Their memory stores are independent and do not conflict.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
**MCP in Open WebUI:** Native HTTP/SSE MCP support since **v0.6.31**. For stdio-based MCP servers (filesystem, memory, brave-search, etc.) the `mcpo` proxy converts them to OpenAPI endpoints. Section 6 covers both paths. nanobot reuses the same MCP server definitions without the proxy.
:::

---

# WezTerm

WezTerm is a GPU-accelerated cross-platform terminal emulator used for all commands in this guide.

## Install

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Windows

```powershell
winget install wez.wezterm
```

Or download from [wezfurlong.org/wezterm/installation](https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/installation.html).

### macOS

```bash
brew install --cask wezterm
```

### Linux (Debian/Ubuntu/Zorin)

```bash
curl -fsSL https://apt.fury.io/wez/gpg.key \
  | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/wezterm-fury.gpg
echo 'deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/wezterm-fury.gpg] https://apt.fury.io/wez/ * *' \
  | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/wezterm.list
sudo apt update && sudo apt install wezterm
```

:::

## Verify

```bash
wezterm --version
```

---

# pixi

pixi manages Python environments without conda or venv friction. Install once globally.

## Install

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Windows

```powershell
winget install prefix-dev.pixi
```

### macOS

```bash
brew install pixi
```

### Linux

```bash
curl -fsSL https://pixi.sh/install.sh | sh
source ~/.bashrc
```

:::

## Verify

```bash
pixi --version
```

---

# Ollama

Ollama runs open-weight LLMs locally. Open WebUI, nanobot, and OpenSpace all connect to it.

## Install

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Windows

Download from [ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download). The installer registers Ollama as a background service automatically.

### macOS

```bash
brew install ollama
brew services start ollama
```

### Linux

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
# Registers and starts a systemd service automatically — runs on boot.
```

:::

## Verify

```bash
ollama --version
ollama list          # empty initially
```

## Pull a baseline model

```bash
ollama pull gemma3:12b
```

::: {.callout-tip}
On lower VRAM (under 8 GB), use `gemma3:4b` or `qwen2.5:7b` instead.
:::

## Test

```bash
ollama run gemma3:12b "What is a skill-based memory agent? Two sentences."
```

---

# Open WebUI

Chat interface connecting Ollama locally, any OpenAI-compatible API externally, and MCP tool servers natively since v0.6.31.

## Install

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Windows / macOS

```bash
mkdir ~/openwebui && cd ~/openwebui
pixi init
pixi add python==3.11 pip
pixi run pip install open-webui
```

### Linux

```bash
mkdir ~/openwebui && cd ~/openwebui
pixi init
pixi add python==3.11 pip
pixi run pip install open-webui
# Note the binary path — needed for the systemd unit in Section 13:
pixi run which open-webui
```

:::

## Start (manual / development)

::: {.panel-tabset}

### macOS / Linux

```bash
cd ~/openwebui
MCP_ENABLE=true pixi run open-webui serve
```

For permanent operation on Linux, use the systemd unit in Section 13 instead.

### Windows

```powershell
$env:MCP_ENABLE = "true"
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run open-webui serve
```

:::

Open `http://localhost:8080`. On first launch it auto-detects Ollama at `http://localhost:11434`.

::: {.callout-note}
`MCP_ENABLE=true` is required to show the MCP connections panel. First start downloads a small embedding model — allow 1–2 minutes.
:::

## Connect external APIs

After completing the API key sections below, go to **Settings → Connections** and add:

| Provider | Base URL | Key |
|---|---|---|
| OpenRouter | `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1` | `sk-or-v1-...` |
| Gemini | `https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/` | `AIza-...` |
| Perplexity | `https://api.perplexity.ai` | `pplx-...` |

---

# MCP Tools

MCP lets the agent call external tools — filesystem access, web search, memory stores, databases, and more.

| Transport | Examples | Setup needed |
|---|---|---|
| **HTTP / SSE** | Remote services, n8n, hosted MCP | Register URL directly in UI |
| **stdio** (most common) | filesystem, memory, brave-search | `mcpo` proxy → register in Open WebUI |

nanobot connects to stdio MCP servers directly without mcpo — see Section 11.

## Path A — Native HTTP MCP (Open WebUI only)

1. **Admin Settings → External Tools → + Add Server**
2. Type: **MCP (Streamable HTTP)**
3. Paste the server URL and auth token → **Save**

## Path B — stdio MCP via mcpo (Open WebUI)

### Install mcpo

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Windows / macOS / Linux

```bash
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run pip install mcpo
# Note binary path for systemd (Linux):
pixi run which mcpo
```

:::

### Create `mcp-config.json`

Create `~/openwebui/mcp-config.json`. This same file is also referenced by nanobot in Section 11.

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "filesystem": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": [
        "-y",
        "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem",
        "/home/youruser/Documents"
      ]
    },
    "memory": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": [
        "-y",
        "@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory"
      ]
    },
    "brave-search": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": [
        "-y",
        "@modelcontextprotocol/server-brave-search"
      ],
      "env": {
        "BRAVE_API_KEY": "your-brave-api-key-here"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Adjust the filesystem path for your OS:

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Linux

```json
"/home/youruser/Documents"
```

#### macOS

```json
"/Users/yourname/Documents"
```

#### Windows

```json
"C:\\Users\\yourname\\Documents"
```

:::

::: {.callout-note}
Node.js is required for `npx`. Check with `node --version`.

**Install if missing:**

```bash
# Linux (nvm — recommended)
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.1/install.sh | bash
source ~/.bashrc && nvm install --lts

# macOS
brew install node

# Windows
winget install OpenJS.NodeJS.LTS
```
:::

### Start mcpo (manual)

```bash
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run mcpo --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 \
  --config ./mcp-config.json --hot-reload
```

On Linux, use the systemd unit in Section 13 instead.

### Verify

```bash
curl http://localhost:8000/docs
# Returns OpenAPI schema for all registered servers
```

### Register in Open WebUI

**Admin Settings → External Tools → + Add Server → Type: OpenAPI**

```
http://localhost:8000/filesystem
http://localhost:8000/memory
http://localhost:8000/brave-search
```

---

# OpenRouter

Unified access to 200+ cloud models under a single endpoint.

## Get your API key

1. Sign up at [openrouter.ai](https://openrouter.ai) → **Keys → Create key**
2. Add credits under **Credits** (pay-as-you-go)

## Set the environment variable

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Linux / macOS

```bash
# Add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc
export OPENROUTER_API_KEY="sk-or-v1-your-key-here"
source ~/.bashrc
```

### Windows

```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(
  "OPENROUTER_API_KEY", "sk-or-v1-your-key-here", "User"
)
```

:::

## Test

```bash
curl https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENROUTER_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"google/gemini-2.0-flash-001","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}'
```

---

# Gemini API

Primary reasoning engine for OpenSpace. Billed separately from your Gemini Advanced subscription.

::: {.callout-important}
**Gemini Advanced** covers the chat product only. Get an API key from AI Studio — generous free tier, paid via Google Cloud billing.
:::

## Get your API key

1. Go to [aistudio.google.com](https://aistudio.google.com)
2. **Get API key → Create API key** → copy `AIza...`

## Set the environment variable

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Linux / macOS

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="AIza-your-key-here"
```

### Windows

```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(
  "GEMINI_API_KEY", "AIza-your-key-here", "User"
)
```

:::

## Test

```bash
curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-2.0-flash:generateContent?key=$GEMINI_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"contents":[{"parts":[{"text":"Say hello in one word"}]}]}'
```

---

# Perplexity API

Adds live web-search to the agent via `sonar-pro`. Pro subscription includes $5/month in API credits — billed separately from the chat product.

::: {.callout-important}
Enable API billing at [perplexity.ai/settings/api](https://perplexity.ai/settings/api) — the chat subscription and API are independent.
:::

## Get your API key

1. Go to [perplexity.ai/settings/api](https://perplexity.ai/settings/api)
2. **Generate** → copy `pplx-...`

## Set the environment variable

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Linux / macOS

```bash
export PERPLEXITY_API_KEY="pplx-your-key-here"
```

### Windows

```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(
  "PERPLEXITY_API_KEY", "pplx-your-key-here", "User"
)
```

:::

## Test

```bash
curl https://api.perplexity.ai/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $PERPLEXITY_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"sonar","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"What is todays date?"}]}'
```

---

# OpenSpace

Self-evolving skill engine. Extracts a reusable skill after each task and reuses it on subsequent similar tasks — cutting token cost and latency over time.

**Repo:** [github.com/HKUDS/OpenSpace](https://github.com/HKUDS/OpenSpace)

## How it works

```
User task
    │
    ▼
Skill Retrieval ──► ~/.openspace_skills.json
    │                  hit: reuse skill
    │                  miss: reason from scratch
    ▼
Gemini 2.5 Flash          ← OPENSPACE_MODEL in .env
    │
    ├── needs web data? ──► web_search() ──► Perplexity sonar-pro
    │
    ▼
Result + new skill extracted and saved
```

## Create the project

```bash
mkdir ~/openspace-agent && cd ~/openspace-agent
pixi init
pixi add python==3.11 pip
pixi run pip install python-dotenv
pixi run pip install "git+https://github.com/HKUDS/OpenSpace.git"
```

## Create `.env`

```ini
OPENSPACE_MODEL=gemini/gemini-2.5-flash
GEMINI_API_KEY=AIza-your-key-here
PERPLEXITY_API_KEY=pplx-your-key-here
```

```bash
echo ".env" >> .gitignore
```

## Create `tools.py`

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI

_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.environ["PERPLEXITY_API_KEY"],
    base_url="https://api.perplexity.ai",
)

def web_search(query: str) -> str:
    """Live web search via Perplexity sonar-pro. Call when current information is needed."""
    resp = _client.chat.completions.create(
        model="sonar-pro",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}],
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content
```

## Verify

```python
# verify.py
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()

from openspace import OpenSpace
from tools import web_search

agent = OpenSpace(tools=[web_search])
print(agent.run("What is 2 + 2? Answer in exactly one word."))
# Expected: "Four"
```

```bash
pixi run python verify.py
```

---

# Jumpstart Skills

Seeds the skill library with 21 pre-distilled skills covering R coding, ggplot2, SKAT, bibliometrics, fact-checking, and 16 other domains.

## Place the seed files

```
~/openspace-agent/
├── .env
├── .gitignore
├── tools.py
├── verify.py
├── seed_skills.py
└── openspace_skills.json
```

## Run the seeder

```bash
cd ~/openspace-agent
pixi run python seed_skills.py
# ✓ Added 21 new skills (21 total in library)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
To reset to the seed set only:

```bash
pixi run python seed_skills.py --replace
```
:::

## Skills installed

| Domain | Skills |
|---|---|
| Dev / Code | Prompt engineering, R coding, ggplot2, CLI tooling, Oracle/DB |
| FOSS | FOSS alternatives, Linux/Debian troubleshooting |
| Research | Fact-checking, bibliometrics, demography |
| Danish context | SKAT/tax, press weekly, daily news, writing assistant |
| Information tasks | AI briefing, public figure profiling, OCR+translate, alt text |
| Personal | Running coach (Garmin CSV), health/medical |
| Lifestyle | Walking tour design |

---

# nanobot

nanobot is a lightweight agent runtime (~4,000 lines) from the same HKUDS lab as OpenSpace. It handles the always-on conversational layer that OpenSpace does not cover: Telegram messages from your phone, scheduled briefings, and background subagent tasks. It reuses all your existing API keys and MCP server definitions.

**Repo:** [github.com/HKUDS/nanobot](https://github.com/HKUDS/nanobot)

## What it adds vs what you already have

| Capability | OpenSpace | Open WebUI | nanobot |
|---|---|---|---|
| Browser chat UI | — | ✅ | — |
| **Telegram / phone chat** | — | — | ✅ |
| Skill-learning memory | ✅ | — | own simpler system |
| MCP tools | via tools.py | via mcpo | native stdio |
| **Scheduled tasks / cron** | — | — | ✅ |
| **Subagents / background jobs** | — | — | ✅ |
| Ollama / local models | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

::: {.callout-note}
**Dual memory stores:** nanobot keeps its own conversational memory in `~/.nanobot/`. OpenSpace keeps skill memory in `~/.openspace_skills.json`. They are independent and do not conflict — treat them as different layers: nanobot = chat context, OpenSpace = reusable task patterns.
:::

## Create a Telegram bot

1. Open Telegram and search for **@BotFather**
2. Send `/newbot` and follow the prompts
3. Copy the **bot token** (`123456:ABC-...`)
4. Find your Telegram user ID: send any message to **@userinfobot** → copy your `id`

## Install

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Windows / macOS / Linux

```bash
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run pip install nanobot-ai
pixi run nanobot onboard
# Note the binary path for systemd (Linux):
pixi run which nanobot
```

:::

## Create `~/.nanobot/config.json`

nanobot shares your existing API keys and reuses the `mcpServers` block directly from your `mcp-config.json` — copy it in:

```json
{
  "providers": {
    "openrouter": {
      "apiKey": "sk-or-v1-your-key-here"
    },
    "gemini": {
      "apiKey": "AIza-your-key-here"
    },
    "ollama": {
      "apiKey": "no-key",
      "apiBase": "http://localhost:11434/v1"
    }
  },
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "model": "openrouter/google/gemini-2.5-flash"
    }
  },
  "tools": {
    "mcpServers": {
      "filesystem": {
        "command": "npx",
        "args": [
          "-y",
          "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem",
          "/home/youruser/Documents"
        ]
      },
      "memory": {
        "command": "npx",
        "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-memory"]
      },
      "brave-search": {
        "command": "npx",
        "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-brave-search"],
        "env": {
          "BRAVE_API_KEY": "your-brave-api-key-here"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "channels": {
    "telegram": {
      "enabled": true,
      "token": "YOUR_TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN",
      "allowFrom": ["YOUR_TELEGRAM_USER_ID"]
    }
  }
}
```

::: {.callout-warning}
`allowFrom` restricts the bot to your Telegram user ID only. Do not leave it empty — any Telegram user could otherwise send commands to your agent and reach your filesystem.
:::

## Start the gateway (manual)

```bash
pixi run nanobot gateway
```

Send a message to your bot in Telegram — it should respond immediately.

For permanent operation on Linux, use the systemd unit in Section 13 instead.

## Test via CLI

```bash
pixi run nanobot agent -m "What files are in my Documents folder?"
pixi run nanobot agent -m "Search the web for the latest Ollama release"
```

## Add scheduled tasks

Scheduled tasks run on cron syntax and deliver results to your Telegram chat automatically:

```bash
# Daily 8am briefing
pixi run nanobot cron add \
  --name "morning" \
  --message "Give me a 5-bullet morning briefing: weather in Copenhagen, top tech news, one interesting paper in demography" \
  --cron "0 8 * * *"

# Monday weekly press summary
pixi run nanobot cron add \
  --name "weekly-press" \
  --message "Summarise the top 5 Danish news stories from the past week" \
  --cron "0 9 * * 1"

# List all scheduled tasks
pixi run nanobot cron list

# Remove a task
pixi run nanobot cron remove --name "morning"
```

---

# Persistent Services (Linux)

Keeps the full stack running in the background and surviving reboots. Ollama is already registered as a systemd service by its installer. Create units for Open WebUI, mcpo, and nanobot.

::: {.callout-note}
This section is Linux-only (systemd). On **macOS**, use `brew services` for Ollama and `launchd` plists for the rest. On **Windows**, use Task Scheduler (run on login, hidden window).
:::

## Find pixi binary paths

```bash
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run which open-webui   # e.g. /home/youruser/.pixi/envs/default/bin/open-webui
pixi run which mcpo         # e.g. /home/youruser/.pixi/envs/default/bin/mcpo
pixi run which nanobot      # e.g. /home/youruser/.pixi/envs/default/bin/nanobot
```

Replace the `ExecStart` paths in all units below with your actual output.

## Open WebUI — `/etc/systemd/system/openwebui.service`

```ini
[Unit]
Description=Open WebUI
After=network.target ollama.service
Wants=ollama.service

[Service]
Type=simple
User=youruser
WorkingDirectory=/home/youruser/openwebui
EnvironmentFile=/home/youruser/openwebui/.env.service
Environment="MCP_ENABLE=true"
ExecStart=/home/youruser/.pixi/envs/default/bin/open-webui serve
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

## mcpo — `/etc/systemd/system/mcpo.service`

```ini
[Unit]
Description=mcpo MCP proxy
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=youruser
WorkingDirectory=/home/youruser/openwebui
ExecStart=/home/youruser/.pixi/envs/default/bin/mcpo \
  --host 0.0.0.0 \
  --port 8000 \
  --config /home/youruser/openwebui/mcp-config.json \
  --hot-reload
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

## nanobot — `/etc/systemd/system/nanobot.service`

```ini
[Unit]
Description=nanobot agent gateway
After=network.target ollama.service

[Service]
Type=simple
User=youruser
WorkingDirectory=/home/youruser/openwebui
ExecStart=/home/youruser/.pixi/envs/default/bin/nanobot gateway
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

## Create the shared environment file

Create `/home/youruser/openwebui/.env.service` (plain `KEY=value`, no `export`):

```ini
GEMINI_API_KEY=AIza-your-key-here
OPENROUTER_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-your-key-here
PERPLEXITY_API_KEY=pplx-your-key-here
```

```bash
chmod 600 ~/openwebui/.env.service
```

## Enable and start everything

```bash
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now ollama openwebui mcpo nanobot

# Check status
sudo systemctl status ollama openwebui mcpo nanobot

# Live logs
journalctl -fu openwebui
journalctl -fu mcpo
journalctl -fu nanobot
```

## Service management

```bash
# Restart after config changes
sudo systemctl restart openwebui
sudo systemctl restart mcpo
sudo systemctl restart nanobot

# Stop temporarily (autostart remains active)
sudo systemctl stop nanobot

# Disable autostart permanently
sudo systemctl disable nanobot
```

---

# Remote Access

Access Open WebUI from your phone, laptop, or any device from anywhere.

## Option A — Tailscale (Recommended)

Creates a private WireGuard mesh between your devices. Nothing is exposed to the internet. Works through CGNAT (common in Denmark). Free tier: up to 100 devices.

### Install on the home machine

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Linux

```bash
curl -fsSL https://tailscale.com/install.sh | sh
sudo tailscale up
tailscale ip        # note this, e.g. 100.64.1.5
```

#### macOS

```bash
brew install tailscale
sudo tailscale up
tailscale ip
```

#### Windows

```powershell
winget install tailscale.tailscale
# Open the Tailscale tray icon → Log in
```

:::

### Install on every client device

- **Android:** Tailscale on Google Play
- **iOS:** Tailscale on App Store
- **Laptop:** same installer as above

Sign in to the **same Tailscale account** on all devices.

### Access Open WebUI

```
http://<tailscale-ip>:8080
```

Enable **MagicDNS** in the [Tailscale admin console](https://login.tailscale.com/admin) → DNS → Enable MagicDNS. Then use:

```
http://myhomepc:8080
```

### Optional — subnet routing

Access other home network devices (NAS, router, Raspberry Pi) without installing Tailscale on each:

```bash
sudo tailscale up --advertise-routes=192.168.1.0/24
# Approve the subnet in the admin console:
# Machines → your machine → Edit route settings → Approve
```

## Option B — Cloudflare Tunnel

For access from any browser without installing Tailscale on client devices. Requires a domain on Cloudflare (free plan works).

::: {.callout-warning}
Cloudflare Tunnel makes Open WebUI **publicly reachable**. Before enabling: go to **Admin Settings → Users**, disable public registration, keep only your account. Add Cloudflare Access in front of the tunnel (free) as an additional auth gate.
:::

### Install cloudflared

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Linux (Debian/Ubuntu)

```bash
curl -fsSL https://pkg.cloudflare.com/cloudflare-main.gpg \
  | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/cloudflare-main.gpg >/dev/null
echo 'deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/cloudflare-main.gpg] \
  https://pkg.cloudflare.com/cloudflared jammy main' \
  | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/cloudflared.list
sudo apt update && sudo apt install cloudflared
```

#### macOS

```bash
brew install cloudflared
```

#### Windows

```powershell
winget install Cloudflare.cloudflared
```

:::

### Create and route the tunnel

```bash
cloudflared login
cloudflared tunnel create openwebui
cloudflared tunnel route dns openwebui chat.yourdomain.com
```

### Create `/etc/cloudflared/config.yml`

```yaml
tunnel: <your-tunnel-id>
credentials-file: /root/.cloudflared/<your-tunnel-id>.json

ingress:
  - hostname: chat.yourdomain.com
    service: http://localhost:8080
  - service: http_status:404
```

### Install as a system service

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Linux

```bash
sudo cloudflared service install
sudo systemctl enable --now cloudflared
```

#### macOS

```bash
sudo cloudflared service install
sudo launchctl start com.cloudflare.cloudflared
```

#### Windows

```powershell
cloudflared service install
Start-Service cloudflared
```

:::

## Which option to use

| Situation | Use |
|---|---|
| Accessing from your own devices only | **Tailscale** |
| Sharing with someone who cannot install apps | **Cloudflare Tunnel** |
| Behind CGNAT (most Danish ISPs) | Both work — no router config needed |
| Privacy-first, zero public exposure | **Tailscale** |

---

# Smoke Test

Run this after seeding to exercise the full OpenSpace stack end to end:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()

from openspace import OpenSpace
from tools import web_search

agent = OpenSpace(tools=[web_search])

# Should hit the ggplot2 skill (seeded)
r1 = agent.run(
    "Write a minimal ggplot2 dotplot of mpg vs cyl from mtcars. "
    "Horizontal text, viridis colors."
)
print("=== ggplot2 task ===\n", r1)

# Exercises web_search
r2 = agent.run("What is the latest stable release of Ollama?")
print("=== web search task ===\n", r2)

# Second R task — skill retrieval expected, no cold-start
r3 = agent.run(
    "Write a ggplot2 dumbbell chart comparing life expectancy 2000 vs 2020 "
    "for 10 countries using a fake inline tibble."
)
print("=== ggplot2 task 2 (skill retrieval expected) ===\n", r3)
```

Test nanobot from the CLI:

```bash
cd ~/openwebui
pixi run nanobot agent -m "List my scheduled cron tasks"
pixi run nanobot agent -m "What files are in my Documents folder?"
```

---

# Quick Reference

## Environment variables

| Variable | Where set | Value |
|---|---|---|
| `OPENSPACE_MODEL` | `.env` | `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash` |
| `GEMINI_API_KEY` | `.env.service` / system | From AI Studio |
| `PERPLEXITY_API_KEY` | `.env.service` / system | From perplexity.ai |
| `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` | `.env.service` / system | From openrouter.ai |
| `MCP_ENABLE` | `.env.service` / shell | `true` |

## Service endpoints

| Service | Local URL | Remote (Tailscale) |
|---|---|---|
| Open WebUI | `http://localhost:8080` | `http://myhomepc:8080` |
| Ollama API | `http://localhost:11434` | `http://myhomepc:11434` |
| mcpo proxy | `http://localhost:8000` | internal only |
| mcpo OpenAPI docs | `http://localhost:8000/docs` | — |
| nanobot | Telegram bot | Telegram (no URL needed) |

## External links

| Resource | URL |
|---|---|
| Google AI Studio | `https://aistudio.google.com` |
| Perplexity API settings | `https://perplexity.ai/settings/api` |
| OpenRouter keys | `https://openrouter.ai/keys` |
| Tailscale admin | `https://login.tailscale.com/admin` |
| OpenSpace repo | `https://github.com/HKUDS/OpenSpace` |
| nanobot repo | `https://github.com/HKUDS/nanobot` |

## Day-to-day commands

```bash
# Check all service status (Linux)
sudo systemctl status ollama openwebui mcpo nanobot

# Restart after config changes
sudo systemctl restart openwebui mcpo nanobot

# Live logs
journalctl -fu openwebui
journalctl -fu nanobot

# Pull a new Ollama model
ollama pull llama3.2:latest

# Run an OpenSpace task
cd ~/openspace-agent && pixi run python your_script.py

# Re-seed OpenSpace skills (safe to re-run)
cd ~/openspace-agent && pixi run python seed_skills.py

# Manage nanobot scheduled tasks
pixi run nanobot cron list
pixi run nanobot cron add --name "briefing" --message "Morning summary" --cron "0 8 * * *"
pixi run nanobot cron remove --name "briefing"

# Inspect OpenSpace skill library
python -c "
import json, os
lib = json.load(open(os.path.expanduser('~/.openspace_skills.json')))
[print(f'[{k}] {v[\"task\"][:70]}') for k, v in lib.items()]
"

# Check Tailscale status
tailscale status
tailscale ip
```
